data.title: SCHZ: Tariffs More Moderate Than Expected
data.description:
Equity ETFs aren't the only ones that can benefit from the more fair tariffs at the closure of trade deals with major US trading partners. The Schwab U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHZ) is a relatively high duration ETF at just under 6 years in effective duration. We note several important data points this week that resounded in Fed comments that the inflation battle in the US is at long last nearing being over. While there is still some underlying inflation, and tariffs haven't helped, even lagging data is creating a cumulative argument that should see yields come down. Therefore, duration is back on the menu.

The SCHZ Case

The ETF is mostly AA rated because it is 45% US Gov't securities, namely Treasuries. The rest of the exposures are MBS's and corporate bonds. The ratio between AA:A:BBB exposures are around 14:3:3. Talking about credit spreads of AA bonds when most are Treasuries isn't that interesting, but discussing the BBB credit spreads for illustrative purposes, we see that risk taking is at historical highs with BBB credit spreads near historical lows. This is where we would have a bit of an issue with the ETF, as we don't think it's necessarily that valid to believe that risk to credit is particularly low in the current environment. In fact, we think that some of the factors that are helping drive the disinflation case create some cracks in the macroeconomic outlook that could threaten credit spreads a little bit. But the exposure to non AA and A rated bonds in the ETF is little, where AA and A rated bonds are not going to be sensitive to the likely changes in credit spreads we'd be seeing, particularly as macro indicators aren't that poor either thinking particularly about positive below expectations figures of jobless claims and positive nonfarm employment changes.

The case for disinflation is growth in wholesale inventories, which means that the more commodified part of the value chain is seeing a little bit more supply than expected, which is somewhat disinflationary. Even more important than that, there are MoM declines in the house price indices, which are correlated to the general situation in shelter prices, which are trending down. Jobs openings are less than expected, signaling reduced demand for labor than expected, which is also disinflationary as it tackles wage-price dynamics. Though employment change was positive along with today's initial jobless claims figures being less than expected and in line with the previous month's data, indicating a decent economic backdrop as well. Crude inventories were higher than expected which is a little bearish for oil prices, which has a pretty big impact on consumer expectations and usually transmits per $10 per barrel change into CPI by around 0.4%. Critically, the FOMC seems to be indicating that the inflation battle is "most of the way back to 2%". While the Core PCE figure, which is inflation excluding volatile elements (a good thing considering volatility in June in energy prices), is in line with the previous figure and a little higher than expected, it is reflecting some of the dynamics associated with Trump tariff rhetoric that is already in the past. Namely:

  • Copper tariffs seem to be weakened to only including semi-finished products and not affecting ores and other concentrates. This is a pretty big deal because it collapsed the copper trade that has been ongoing since copper tariffs were first announced and caused domestic US copper to be speculated upon massively.
  • Of course the tariffs with Europe, Japan, and South Korea, major US trading partners, as well as a smattering of Southeast Asian trading partners, have concluded with rates all below 20%, and with rates significantly dialed back from the threatened reciprocal tariff rates from "Liberation Day."

The final thing to reference is inflation expectations. Actual inflation expectations of consumers are an incredibly important causative factor for inflation to occur. Measuring it is a bit more sketchy as some of the surveys have shown considerable partisan bias. Relying upon the US NY Fed survey for 1-year out expectations shows that they are moderating a bit, despite the bump caused by the initial wave of tariff rhetoric that consumers were understandably sensitive to. Expectations were at 3.2% in early June as surveyed, and early July they were 3%. We think they might be even lower now.

Bottom Line

Compared to 6 months ago the yield curve has clearly shifted down in the 6 year time horizon. When we last covered duration picks we pointed out that outstanding questions around inflation were still present. Most of those have since been answered, and we believe this is also a reason why dissent is appearing in the FOMC, and some of the more conservative members are now even signaling their belief that it might be time for the Fed to pivot to the growth mandate. Thankfully, solid employment figures indicate that the growth mandate isn't in peril, but the dissent in the FOMC is a clear indicator, particularly from Bowman, that the pernicious effects of anchored and higher inflation are no longer of principle concern as the underlying inflation is sufficiently under control at this point, and cannot flywheel up on wage-price and other guiding inflation expectation effects. Therefore, with the yield curve having actually shifted up a bit this last month rather than down, it might be a tactical moment for duration bets. SCHZ, which just a 0.03% expense ratio and effectively being a Treasury or otherwise low credit risk exposure, is a decent way to make a duration bet with a 6 years duration giving the ETF noticeable sensitivity to changes in the yield curve.

data.title: Intesa Sanpaolo Q2 Earnings: Another Display Of Resilience
data.description:
Italy's Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY) (OTCPK:IITSF) delivered another strong set of results for the second quarter, once again demonstrating that its earnings remain resilient in the face of lower interest rates in the Eurozone. Specifically, not only was Intesa's €2.6 billion bottom line higher than it was a year ago, but this is still mapping to an exceptional 20%-plus return on tangible equity.

This resilience continues to drive the shares higher, too, with Intesa's ADSs returning another 11% since my last update in May. That brings their total year-to-date return to around the 60% mark.

This performance leaves the stock trading for a circa 1.95x multiple of tangible book value. While that looks expensive for a European bank, the quality of Intesa's profitability continues to result in more attractive multiples based on earnings and shareholder distributions. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Q2 2025: More Evidence Of Resilience

For the second quarter of 2025, Intesa booked a net profit of €2.601 billion, around 5% higher than the year-ago period. The bank has achieved this despite sharply lower interest rates in the Eurozone, with the quarterly average 1-month Euribor rate down around 170bps year-on-year last quarter. As Intesa's valuation has been expanding, this resilience has become increasingly important to its investment case.

Last time, I mentioned three drivers that were contributing to this. The first is that, while it's softening, Intesa's net interest income [NII] remains relatively robust, having risen 80% over the past three years.

Looking at the drivers of that, Intesa is the leading deposit gatherer in Italy. While it doesn't disclose its deposit costs, they're likely to be similar to recently-covered peer UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY) (OTCPK:UNCFF). UniCredit paid less than 30bps on its Italian deposits last quarter. Even at the top of the hiking cycle, when the European Central Bank's base rate was 4%, it was only paying around 60bps. As the current 5-year Euro swap rate is around 2.4%, gathering these deposits is therefore a very profitable activity.

Of course, this should also make Intesa quite sensitive to declining interest rates, which would eat into this attractive deposit margin. Last quarter, Intesa generated €3.8 billion in group-wide NII, down around 6% year-on-year. However, NII was actually quite resilient in the Italian business, falling by ~2% year-on-year to €1.717 billion. Furthermore, while down year-on-year, group NII rose modestly quarter-on-quarter, albeit there may be a day-count impact here (Q2 had more days than Q1).

Per last time, the bank's structural hedge contniues to help. As you can see in the image above (in the green fine print), Intesa has hedged around €160 billion in core deposits, disclosing a 4-year duration and a yield of 1.6%. Management has further disclosed that hedge maturities are running at around €2.4 billion per month. Conceptually, this hedge can be viewed as being similar to the bank investing in a portfolio of bonds, funded by the high stock of rate-insensitive deposits I outlined above.

As well as locking in a spread, the above would also imply that current maturities continue to reflect hedges that were taken out back when interest rates were low. As these reprice into the current environment, the hedge yield should continue to rise.

This seems like it's playing out. The current 1.6% yield is up ~10bps from my last piece and ~40bps year-on-year. Given the yield remains below current euro swap rates, this process should presumably continue, which will support Intesa's NII. Indeed, per the earnings call, management expects the hedge to be a positive contributor next year:

So in net interest income is the area in which we are continue to have a very good performance in terms of core hedging facilities. So the contribution of core hedging is very positive -- to be very positive, and our expectation is that we remain positive also during 2026.

Carlo Messina, Managing Director and CEO, Intesa Sanpaolo

Elsewhere, I was looking for fee & commission income to help offset any weakness in NII. Intesa has major lines of business in asset management and insurance, which it can sell to the large customer base gathered by its banking business. Combined, fees and insurance revenue are around 42% of Intesa's top line, one of the highest shares among large European banks. Intesa continues to make solid progress growing these fees; last quarter, fee & insurance revenue came in at €2.909 billion, up 3% year-on-year.

The final driver is expense control. Like many banks, Intesa is seeing technology impact its business, with customers now less reliant on traditional brick-and-mortar branches. This is allowing the bank to extract savings from its cost base, keeping overall expense growth in check. Indeed, costs came in at €2.664 billion last quarter, virtually flat year-on-year.

Asset quality also remains strong. Despite tariff fears, non-performing loans have remained steady at around the €10 billion mark, or around 2.3% of gross balances. Accordingly, the bank booked a provisioning charge of €280 million last quarter, equal to a circa 25bps cost of risk.

The Valuation

Shares of Intesa are trading for €5.29 as I type, which is roughly equal to $36.26 per ADS. That maps to a multiple of around 1.95x the bank's tangible book value at the end of Q2 (€2.70 per share; $18.50 per ADS).

Given we are close to 2x TBV, I don't expect to see much more multiple expansion from here. However, that doesn't mean the stock can't do well. Given Intesa is earning a 20%-plus ROTE, the price/earnings ratio remains under 10x (annualizing H1 2025 EPS of €0.294 would put the multiple at 9x).

This level of profitability is also allowing Intesa to return nearly all of its profits to investors. Indeed, it has already accrued €3.7 billion in dividends related to its H1 2025 performance, while the bank also announced a €2 billion buyback in June. Its total shareholder yield is running at around 9%-10%. Plus, Intesa is returning these sums while maintaining an ample capital cushion.

Per above, Intesa's Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio was 13.5% at the end of Q2, rising to over 14.5% including the benefit it will see from the absorption of deferred tax assets. On the call, management discussed a 12% CET1 ratio. While it will take some time to work down to this level, the bank's excess capital will support its payout potential, implying an ongoing high shareholder yield. Accordingly, investors can realize a good return from dividends and buybacks alone.

Conclusion

Second quarter results offered more proof that Intesa Sanpaolo's earnings remain resilient in the face of lower interest rates, with the bank continuing to earn a stellar 20%-plus return on tangible equity. While the stock's multiple of tangible book value now better reflects Intesa's profitability, its capital cushion and high quality earnings are keeping the shareholder yield attractive. On that basis, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ford Earnings: Why And How I Plan To Sell At $11
data.description:
F stock: FQ2 earnings

I last covered Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) stock on June 3 in an article entitled "Ford Motor: Dividend Safety Has Come Into Question." That article was triggered by its latest dividend declaration at that time. I rated the stock as a hold due to the following concerns:

Ford Motor Company's Q1 earnings showed strong U.S. truck and EV sales, but profit margins remain under pressure vs. peers and history. Despite an attractive 7.4% dividend yield, I see payout sustainability risks due to high payout ratios and earnings headwinds.

Since then, there have been a few important developments, both in terms of its specific business and also macroeconomics. For the former category, the company just released its earnings for FQ2, on July 30. Overall, I keep seeing unevenness and uncertainties ahead. As a reflection, its adjusted EBIT in FQ2, despite a 110% QoQ recovery, is still more than 22% below the same period last few years. In terms of macroeconomics, the Trump administration has announced new trade deals with key trade partners such as Japan and the EU. I feel these new deals will further weaken Ford’s competitive positions, which has led me to the decision of exiting my F position via a call option as elaborated on next.

F stock: why do we want to exit now?

As a brief recap of my F history (and investing strategy too), we hold a small portfolio in a UTMA (Uniform Transfers to Minors) account for our son. It is a very concentrated portfolio– even by our standard – and it has been holding F since 2022. To wit, we first wrote on this account back in July 2022 when we joined the Seeking Alpha platform (see this original post here).

Given our timeframe (or our son’s timeframe) for this account, we hold our positions for years and trade very rarely. As of today, we are still holding three out of those six positions (Apple (AAPL), Goldman Sachs (GS), and F) in our original post. Before moving on, note that our actual portfolio size is less than the $165k shown below. When we first published the portfolio (on July 11, 2022), we set a hypothetical starting size to be $100k, which led to the current portfolio size of $165k.

With this background, let us explain why/how we have changed our mind about F. Over the ~3 years we’ve been holding F, we felt that F’s competitiveness has been gradually weakened and the new tariff rates that have started this year finally made us pull the plug.

As an example, back in 2022, F’s gross margin was similar to overseas competitors such as BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY). But the auto-manufacturing ecosystem in China has advanced materially over the years to a degree that even without the impacts of tariffs, F’s margin fell decisively below BYD’s, as you can see from the following chart. The comparison against key Japanese auto manufacturers such as Toyota (TM) is equally concerning, as shown in the second chart below.

Looking ahead, recent changes in tariff rates have led us to conclude that F’s profit pressure would further worsen. China’s auto manufacturing ecosystem is much more self sufficient than that in the U.S. As such, we fear that China’s automobile manufacturers like BYD will be much less sensitive to auto parts price changes under the new tariffs than US manufacturers such as F. As for its competition against Japanese car makers, the situation is equally gloomy. In particular, the latest US-Japan trade agreement lowers the import tariff on Japanese automobiles to 15% (from the previous 27.5%), but maintains a high tariff of 25% on cars from Canada and Mexico, where a good portion of F’s cars were made. The “big three” from Detroit (F, GM (GM), and Stellantis (STLA)) raised concerns about the Japan trade deal immediately, while Japan’s auto stocks such as Toyota and Honda (HMC) enjoyed some of their best rallies in the Nikkei stock exchange.

F stock: Our exit strategy

In terms of our exit strategy, we used covered-call options. The option premium helps to boost the return a bit (actually quite a bit in this case). As opportunistic investors, we’ve set a limit order at $0.45 per share for F call options with $11 strike and Aug. 1 expiry on back in June (as detailed in this earlier post). The order was triggered a few days later exactly at a price of $0.45 (see the chart below) - speaking of being opportunistic. As of this writing, F shares have been fluctuating closely around $11 and we thus still do not know if our call option will be exercised or not (and thus we're designating a hold rating as we're still holding our shares). If the option is not exercised, we will keep selling calls till our shares are called.

F stock: other risks and final thoughts

The reason for our use of call option in this case is that F’s implied volatility (IV) is quite elevated – for good reasons given the uncertainties mentioned above. As such, the option premium is quite attractive. As you can see from the following chart, F’s implied volatility (IV) is currently 26.9, which puts it in the 12% percentile rank over the past 52 weeks. This means that only 12% of the time the IV was lower in the last year than the current reading, leading to a quite favorable pricing environment for option sellers. For example, our said option above received a premium of $0.45 per share, translating into a yield of almost 4% over ~1 month.

In terms of other risks, the top 2 on our lists are tariffs again (surprise!) and also F’s current valuation. We feel it's not an exaggeration to describe the changes in tariff rates in the past 2~3 months as a rollercoaster ride. As such, we won’t be surprised to see further changes in the near future, which could impact F’s operations and stock price. However, as mentioned earlier, my assessment is that F’s competitive position against overseas players has weakened materially in the past few years regardless of tariffs changes. Also, trading at single-digit P/E ratios (as shown below), F stock is very compressed both in absolute and relative terms, which could be attractive to investors seeking deep value or turnaround opportunities. Finally, note that the use of covered-call options provides me with a fixed upside (the option premium) but entails an unlimited downside in the case F stock prices are substantially above the strike price.

To close, this follow up on F was triggered by recent updates in its specific business and also macroscopic changes. Judging by its earnings for FQ2 just released, I keep seeing large profit uncertainties ahead. The new trade deals announced by the Trump administration (especially with Japan) could further weaken Ford’s competitiveness. These concerns have led us to find an exit of my F shares, and we feel the use of options is a viable approach given the elevated IV.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: Silicon-Anodes Power Aerospace Growth And Upside Potential
data.description:
Amprius Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:AMPX) is a company that develops advanced battery solutions. Their approach leverages silicon-anode lithium-ion technology to deliver high energy density, rapid charging, and robust thermal with relatively safe performance. AMPX's commercial production has been underway since 2018. So far, its technology has been validated and adopted by major aerospace and defense firms like Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), AeroVironment (AVAV), and the U.S. Army. Broadly speaking, AMPX's two core platforms are SiMaxx for ultra-high-performance and specialized applications and SiCore for regular commercial clients.

I previously covered AMPX back in March 2024 and rated them a Buy. Since then, the stock has surged by around 177.7%. Still, even after such an impressive rally, I continue to lean bullish on the shares due to their healthy growth prospects into 2026.

Lithium Energy

Amprius Technologies develops advanced lithium-ion energy storage solutions based on silicon nanowire battery anodes. They've only started commercializing their products since 2018, but they were founded back in 2008. AMPX is currently headquartered in Fremont, California. Having said that, one of their main differentiators is their best-in-class performance in energy density, charging velocity, and thermal range.

Looking ahead, AMPX's management intends to expand into the aerospace and defense markets. They'll adapt their platforms for mainstream battery-powered applications, leveraging the superior capabilities of their silicon-anode battery technologies. In particular, they believe that their batteries could help with industries like AI, robotics, mobile devices, micromobility, and consumer electronics.

You see, AMPX's products have a key advantage as they can reach up to 500 Wh/kg (gravimetric) and 1,300 Wh/L (volumetric) high energy densities. In simpler terms, this translates into longer battery lifetimes and lighter weight for the same energy outputs. AMPX's batteries also support high-rate energy discharges (10 times their capacity in one hour). This is actually a particularly essential requirement for high-drain applications like drones or electric aircraft. AMPX's batteries can also reach 80% recharge in less than 6 minutes and have thermal stability and safety that passed the Military Nail Penetration Test.

Unique Technology With SiMaxx And SiCore

In general, AMPX's two core anode technologies are SiMaxx and SiCore, which are the underlying basis of the rest of its battery product lines. SiMaxx has a pure silicon design with vertical nanowire-like structures that enable the highest energy density. This is the technology that has up to 500 Wh/kg and 1,300 Wh/L for extremely fast charging rates. So, that makes SiMaxx ideal for aerospace, high-altitude pseudo-satellites (HAPS), long-endurance drones, and defense platforms. After all, those applications are often optimized for lightweight and long flight times, as those factors are mission-critical.

Though note that AMPX is still manufacturing these types of batteries in smaller volumes in Fremont and only offers them to specialized customers. Now, as for SiCore, this is a scalable platform that uses 100% silicon or a blend of silicon with other materials. This platform balances performance and commercial manufacturability. SiCore batteries have slightly lower energy density than SiMaxx but are optimized for power delivery, cycle life, and high-volume production. This technology enables products like SiCore 370 to deliver high power output for energy-intensive phases like takeoff and landing.

So, although SiCore is slightly less efficient than SiMaxx, it's still suitable for electric drones, robotics, eVTOLs, and other high-power devices. The main benefit of this platform is that it's highly scalable in terms of production and manufacturing, which is ideal for mass commercialization. SiCore was initially introduced back in January 2024 and has quickly become one of AMPX's main growth drivers with its AMPX customer base. Moreover, AMPX manufactures its batteries both in the U.S. and in South Korea and is evaluating other global manufacturing partners.

In the U.S., AMPX manufactures its products in the Amprius Lab in Fremont, California. They produce up to 2 MWh, primarily for SiMaxx cells. This lab is used for R&D, pilot production, and early-stage commercialization. Its second U.S. facility is in Brighton, Colorado, and AMPX uses it in the design phase for GWh-scale that can eventually meet future demand. Over time, AMPX aims to scale production of SiCore cells to contribute towards a total of 1.8 GWh of contracted manufacturing capacity. Also, AMPX's batteries have been tested and adopted by industry leaders like Airbus' (OTCPK:EADSF) HAPS architecture, with significant purchase orders placed for deliveries through 2025.

In fact, Airbus mentioned that AMPX's batteries are the only energy devices that can enable uninterrupted flight in the stratosphere. Teledyne (TDY) forward-looking infrared (FLIR) has also validated AMPX's batteries, with commercial shipments delivered since Q1 2024 for surveillance and thermal imaging drones. Similarly, AeroVironment (AVAV) uses AMPX's batteries since 2022 through 2025 for tactical and long-range drone systems. And in 2024, AMPX won a competitive xTech award and a Prime contract with the U.S. Army. The company has served more than 280 customers since Q1 2024, with active commercial programs, strategic investments, and government contracts validating AMPX's platforms.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, AMPX trades at a $845.0 million market cap, so it's still shy of that billion-dollar valuation threshold. Its latest Q1 report shows its balance sheet holds $48.4 million in cash against no financial debt (aside from regular operating liabilities). Their book value also reached $70.4 million, indicating an expensive P/B of about 12.0. For comparison, their sector's median P/B is much lower at just 3.0.

Still, I do believe it's fair to assign a "growth" premium to AMPX. After all, Seeking Alpha's dashboard projects they'll generate $180.7 million in revenues by 2026. That implies a forward P/S of 4.7. And even though this is still higher than its peers' median forward P/S of 1.6, it represents a considerable YoY growth of 209.2% by 2026. In contrast, its sector's forward topline growth is much more sluggish at just 4.7%. So, when you factor in AMPX's impressive growth prospects, its valuation today makes much more sense.

Nevertheless, I should mention that AMPX is still burning through its cash reserves. I estimate that in Q1 2025, AMPX had a cash burn of approximately $15.0 million. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. At that rate, it implies a short cash runway of just 3.2 quarters, which is definitely concerning. Of course, as its revenues increase into 2026, its cash burn should decrease, extending its runway. But if such growth fails to materialize, then AMPX will most likely be forced to do another equity raise to continue its operations.

Not only that, but the market would probably erase its "growth" valuation premium as well at the same time. So, if their 2026 growth doesn't materialize, AMPX would suffer a double whammy, as it would probably have to do another equity raise at a much lower valuation. That would translate into an even higher dilution impact. This is why I do concede that AMPX remains inherently speculative at this stage, at least until we see their revenue ramp up as anticipated. After that, I believe its business model will be much more validated, and with it, its investment thesis should be significantly less risky.

That brings us to the key factors we need to keep a close eye on for their next earnings report on August 7, 2025. Most importantly, I think we need to see continued improvement on their gross margins. For example, Q1 2025's gross margins were -20.9%, which was a huge improvement relative to Q1 2024's gross margins of -190.3%. As long as we see this trend continue with its topline growth, then it means AMPX's growth strategy remains in the right direction. Conversely, if we see a meaningful margin deterioration, I think that would throw a big question mark on their long-term sustainability.

Another variable to consider is that AMPX outsources 1.8 GWh in SiCore capacity through Berzelius (and other manufacturing agreements). However, Berzelius is a Chinese company, so I wonder if we'll see any commentary regarding the US tariff and trade policies with China, as well as their potential impacts on AMPX's growth trajectory (and margins). And as for its 500 Wh/kg SiMaxx cells, we should get another update regarding their pilot programs. Ideally, the quicker these programs move forward, the sooner we'll see them play out as AMPX's second growth leg in 2026.

And of course, if there are any delays with these programs, then it could hit its 2026 growth prospects, and by extension, its valuation multiples. Lastly, another key variable to consider will be the upcoming Colorado factory. In Q1, management mentioned they halted those plans, likely due to the CAPEX required to bring it online. But I suspect that if management has a particularly promising Q2, they might again offer a timeline for their Colorado facility. Even a mention of a timeline in 2026 for its construction would be a very positive green flag, as it would show that their demand metrics are even better than anticipated.

Conclusion: Still A Great "Buy"

Overall, AMPX is performing great on all angles. It's obviously still executing on its growth strategy for 2026, which requires balancing its cash balances and CAPEX to make sure its growth doesn't slow down without running dangerously low on cash. This remains why I can't give it a "Strong Buy," since a slight miscalculation could force them into another capital raise or debt. Nevertheless, I believe the most important factors remain favorable. AMPX's demand for its core platforms (SiCore and SiMaxx) is strong. And the underlying secular tailwinds in drones and EVs are likely long-term factors that I believe justify AMPX's inherent risks. Thus, I reiterate a Buy rating on AMPX at these levels, even after its impressive 296% rally from its 2025 lows.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Modine Manufacturing Company 2026 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Modine Manufacturing Company in conjunction with their 2026 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Franklin Municipal Enhanced Income SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: There were large swings in financial market sentiment across the second quarter of 2025. In early April, US President Donald Trump announced significant increases in US tariff rates as part of his “Liberation Day.” Subsequently, a temporary pause (in many cases 90 days) in increased levies helped calm some of the market nerves. The passage of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package after quarter-end further reduced the uncertainties that had been a strong headwind to municipal (muni) bond markets as it affirmed the tax-exempt status of muni securities.
  • Contributors: Overweight high-quality muni bonds. Underweight muni bonds with 15 to 30 years to maturity.
  • Detractors: There were no significant detractors from returns.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive to us, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis, with yields near multi-year highs. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and potentially preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • Our exposure to higher-quality bonds helped relative returns as lower-rated securities, such as BBB rated bonds (which was the worst-performing rating category), underperformed during the quarter.
  • Our underweight to bonds with 15 to 30 years to maturity contributed to results as muni bond yields rose in this portion of the muni yield curve over the quarter.

Outlook

  • We still feel that technical conditions in the market favor investors. Fund flows have moved to be positive, and the additional supply of munis has been well absorbed. The summer months typically see an increase in coupon payments and maturing bonds, which will require reinvestment that should provide a strong base to support valuations.
  • Fundamentals remain broadly stable, with Moody's reporting that credit upgrades continued to outpace downgrades. However, as the economy slows and inflationary pressures ease, tax revenue growth at the state and local level is expected to moderate, placing greater importance on credit selectivity going forward.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive to us, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and potentially preserving portfolio quality.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. An investment in this strategy can lose value. Please visit www.franklintempleton.com for the latest performance figures. Investors cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Fees: Periods less than one year are not annualized. Performance results are for the composite which includes all actual, fully discretionary accounts with substantially similar investment policies and objectives managed to the composite's investment strategy. Composite returns are stated in U.S. dollars and assume reinvestment of any dividends, interest income, capital gains, or other earnings. The composite may include account(s) that are gross of fees and pure gross of fees. "Pure" gross-of-fee returns do not reflect the deduction of any expenses, including transaction costs. A traditional (or "true") gross-of-fee return reflects performance after the reduction of transaction costs but before the reduction of the investment advisory fee. The gross-of-fee return may include a blend of “true” gross-of-fee returns for non-wrap accounts and “pure” gross-of-fee returns for wrap accounts. Net-of-fee returns is reduced by a model “wrap fee” (3.0% is the maximum anticipated wrap fee for equity and balanced portfolios) which includes trading expenses as well as investment management, administrative and custodial fees. The model wrap fee used represents the highest anticipated wrap fee applicable to the strategy. Actual fees and account minimums may vary.

For fee schedules, contact your financial professional, or if you enter into an agreement directly with Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC ("FTPPG"), refer to FTPPG's Form ADV Part 2A disclosure document. Management and performance of individual accounts may vary for reasons that include the existence of different implementation practices and model requirements in different investment programs.

Franklin Templeton claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein. To obtain specific information on available products and services or a GIPS Report, contact your Franklin Templeton separately managed account sales team at (800) DIAL BEN/342-5236.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Investments in underlying funds are subject to the same risks as, and indirectly bear the fees and expenses of, the underlying funds. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. Portfolios focused on a single state are subject to greater risk of adverse economic and regulatory changes than a geographically diversified portfolio.

Glossary

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or portfolio. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the portfolio's selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

These materials are being provided for illustrative and informational purposes only. The information contained herein is obtained from multiple sources that are believed to be reliable. However, such information has not been verified, and may be different from the information included in documents and materials created by the sponsor firm in whose investment program a client participates. Some sponsor firms may require that these materials be preceded or accompanied by investment profiles or other documents or materials prepared by such sponsor firms, which will be provided upon a client's request. For additional information, documents and/or materials, please speak to your Financial Professional or contact your sponsor firm.

Franklin Templeton (FT) is not undertaking to provide impartial advice. Nothing herein is intended to provide fiduciary advice. FT has a financial interest.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a broad measure of the municipal bond market with maturities of at least one year. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes.

Separately Managed Accounts (SMAs) are investment services provided by Franklin Templeton Private Portfolio Group, LLC (FTPPG), a federally registered investment advisor. Client portfolios are managed based on investment instructions or advice provided by affiliated subadvisors of Franklin Templeton. Management is implemented by FTPPG, the designated subadvisor or, in the case of certain programs, the program sponsor or its designee.

data.title: Houston American Energy: Transformative Cleantech Pivot With Cautious Investment Outlook
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Esxeleryn Analytics as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Houston American Energy Corp. (NYSE:HUSA) is no longer a floundering oil and gas relic. After its reverse merger with Abundia Global Impact Group, HUSA has rebranded itself as a cleantech disruptor. The company is targeting the high-growth market for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF). At the center of this shift is a $100 million capital injection and the development of a strategic pyrolysis facility at Cedar Port, Texas. The potential for high value creation for investors is contingent on successful execution of this plant, especially considering this project is funded by a highly dilutive institutional equity line. The outcome hinges entirely on the new management's capability to counter huge execution risk and scale up a commercially ready technology to drive positive cash flow before the dilution loop from its current financing structure could permanently impair shareholder capital.

My thesis is that investment in HUSA stock at this point is a high-stakes binary argument, offering either an explosive upside or a potential value wipeout. The sections that follow present both sides of the argument in detail. Overall, I assign a Hold rating and suggest that potential investors (high-risk takers) should not look beyond taking a small entry position for now.

Cleantech Shift: A Key Stock Price Catalyst

The main catalyst that can lift the stock price is the company's strategic shift from a failing traditional oil and gas firm as a non-operating joint interest owner into a vertically integrated producer of low-carbon fuels and chemicals. This shift is in progress through the acquisition of Abundia Global Impact Group (AGIG) on July 1, 2025. This transition may bring about a change in the company's business operations, target market, and valuation base. The acquisition is a reverse merger, with AGIG’s unitholders receiving shares equivalent to 94% of the post-issuance outstanding stock.

The acquisition essentially makes HUSA the public market vehicle for Abundia’s established technology. This structure provides Abundia (a previously private entity) with immediate access to public capital markets. Logically, this structural move should help accelerate AGIG’s growth plans. By extension, the market could begin to re-rate HUSA stock from a struggling micro-cap energy producer with declining revenues and fully impaired international assets to a growth-oriented company operating in the alternative energy space. I'd like to note that the market assigns a higher valuation multiple to the promising cleantech sector.

The explicit targeting of the multi-billion dollar market for renewable fuels (mainly sustainable aviation fuel, or SAF) places the company within a growth trend supported by global decarbonization mandates and ESG initiatives.

$8.5 Million Cedar Port Acquisition Drives Scalable Growth in Plastics Recycling

A driver for capturing this business growth is the acquisition and planned development of the 25-acre site at Cedar Port Industrial Park in Baytown, Texas, for $8.5 million. This strategic move serves as a foundation for HUSA's first plastics recycling plant, aided by the location's logistical synergies and cost efficiencies. To be specific, Cedar Port is the largest master-planned rail and barge-served industrial park in the United States. The interchange with both Union Pacific and BNSF Railways handles ~18 million carloads annually and has access to two barge terminals on the Houston Ship Channel. I therefore believe the park’s location creates a clear advantage by minimizing transportation costs for both incoming plastic waste feedstock and outgoing pyrolysis oil products.

Further, the park’s location within the Gulf Coast energy corridor may provide easy access to specialized resources (i.e., personnel) in engineering and plant operations. This in turn can help de-risk the operational execution of constructing and running the facility. After commissioning, the plant could serve as a proof-of-concept validating the commercial readiness of pyrolysis technology. Creating such a blueprint for scalable projects can then be a signal to the market, and it can push the stock price up.

Moving to the financial architecture to fund this transformation, the establishment of a $100 million equity line of credit with an institutional investor on July 11, 2025, may serve as a capital reservoir for HUSA's growth strategy. While it is dilutive for current stockholders, this facility is still a must-have as it offers the company the financial flexibility to draw down capital at its discretion to fund the Cedar Port development and scale its operations, bypassing time-consuming and expensive one-off financing rounds.

$100 Million Credit Line Signals Strong Institutional Backing for 2025 Growth

An interesting aspect of this financing structure is the $2 million cap per drawdown. $100 million is not a one-time capital shot but systematic. Simply put, this is metered capital deployment that is in line with project milestones. The point that I want to make with this financial structure is that the securing of such a big line of credit (considering the size of current equity) reflects a high level of institutional (aka smart money) confidence in the new management team and the strategic plan itself.

If managed well, this capital can bridge the company from its current pre-revenue status (in the new venture) to a state of positive operational cash flow. This will be a good mark for the stock's valuation based on tangible earnings. To that end, the upcoming Q2 2025 earnings will serve as a vital gauge, and I will monitor updates on the Cedar Port project and financing-related signals.

To sum up the bullish argument, the shift in the company's institutional ownership profile is an important factor that, I believe, investors should consider. A rotation in the shareholder base during Q1 2025 alongside the lead-up to the AGIG acquisition is the catching point, for me. While one must not ignore that some firms like Squarepoint and Virtu Financial liquidated their positions, new positions were initiated by event-driven (specialized) funds like Sabby Management, LLC (added 185,860 shares) and Citadel Advisors LLC (added 59,815 shares). Along with that, giants like Vanguard Group Inc. also increased the stake by 169,945 shares (a 46.2% rise).

This pattern is suggesting a new class of investors more sophisticated in valuing transformative growth and special situations are entering the fray. This change in the shareholder roster is a signal for me that more stable, long-term-oriented investment enthusiasts with the new multi-year strategy can reduce short-term volatility. So, this is a supporting element for me to conclude a possible higher valuation if the company executes its plan properly.

Dilutive Financing Threatens HUSA Stock Price with Ongoing Shareholder Erosion

Switching gears and moving to the bearish side of the story, the one problem moving forward that can put severe downward pressure on HUSA stock price is the repeated use of dilutive financing. These capital decisions can erode shareholder value faster than the new business can create it. For instance, the very structure of the AGIG acquisition (AGIG members received 94% of the combined company) resulted in massive immediate dilution for pre-existing HUSA shareholders. The acquisition is reducing their stake in the enterprise to a mere fraction of what they held earlier size.

Beyond this initial reset, the company’s financing strategy will push forward the ongoing dilution. The $100 million equity line of credit provides required capital. However, it allows the institutional investor to purchase shares at a 4% discount to the volume-weighted average price (VWAP). This creates a continuous selling pressure as investors can systematically sell shares into the market to lock in a profit. The bad side is that this kind of arrangement can effectively place a cap on any rapid price appreciation with a slow but steady increase in the share count.

Further a value-destructive financing can be observed in the terms of the $5 million convertible note secured on July 11, 2025. The note carries an 8% Original Issue Discount (OID). This means that HUSA received less than the $5.4 million face value of the debt. This immediately reduces the net capital available for the Cedar Port project. The 7% interest rate adds to the cash burn. Most importantly, the convertibility feature presents a large overhang. The note is convertible into common stock at a price that represents a 10% premium to a look-back price that includes, as one criteria, the closing price of $11 on July 10, 2025. This provision clearly incentivizes the noteholder to convert and sell shares if the stock price rises. So obviously, this can cap stock price rallies and flood the trading volume with new supply. I want to refer to these provisions as a classic "death spiral" case.

Even at a smaller level, positive stock price momentum will be met with an increase in the shares for sale. The two registered direct offerings in June 2025 point to this pattern. The offering of 223,762 shares at $10.60 and the subsequent offering of 81,629 shares at $14.80 were relatively small raises but with high costs. The costs here include an 8% fee to the placement agent (Univest Securities, LLC). Also, the timing of these offerings (particularly the one announced on June 18) immediately followed a price surge dampened the momentum, leading to a sharp decline in the share price. This pattern of capitalizing on upward stock movement to raise cash puts a big cost on shareholder returns.

High Execution Risk in Business Model Shift, With Cash Burn Threatening Stock Price Stability

The second risk that I see is the high execution uncertainty within the company's complete business model shift. HUSA has transitioned from being a passive, non-operating interest holder in oil wells to becoming an active developer, constructor, and operator of a complex industrial facility. The previous business model did not go well, and the failure led to a staggering accumulated deficit of $86.2 million as of March 31, 2025 and a full impairment of its Colombian Hupecol Meta investment at year-end 2024. As far as I have assessed, the company has no track record in developing/operating pyrolysis plants, establishing complex supply chains for plastic waste feedstock, or marketing specialized chemical products. Management made claims like "proven pyrolysis process" and "commercially ready solution" in its press release but those need more independent scrutiny, in my opinion.

The journey from acquiring a 25-acre plot of land to establishing a fully operational, profitable plant is fraught with potential pitfalls like construction delays, budget overruns, regulatory issues, and sudden technical issues in scaling the technology. Any material setback in the Cedar Port project timeline/budget will require additional capital (likely the same as the current dilutive financing structures) that can push the stock price down. This may lead to an accelerating erosion in shareholder value because the market is efficient, in addition to being always right, and will scrutinize HUSA’s every step. Thus, any perceived failure to execute the plan can lead to a rapid loss of smart money’s confidence and may lead to a big decline in the stock price (as a result of large stock position exits).

Overall, I believe the company's financials are currently weak for this business model transition. Net losses have widened from $15,699 in Q1 2024 to $1,032,483 in Q1 2025. The jump in losses is based on over $1 million in general and admin expenses related to the acquisition. Similarly, cash flow from operations was a negative $1.3 million for Q1 2025. This reflects a considerable rate of cash burn even before major construction begins.

So, the company's survival and growth are mostly dependent on its access to external capital. This dependency is making the HUSA stock price highly vulnerable to shifts in market sentiment. For instance, if HUSA fails to meet early project expectations, its capability to draw on its equity line and secure new financing can be compromised. This could create a liquidity crisis that will be catastrophic for the stock price.

Technicals View: HUSA Stock Price To Consolidate Until Catalyst-Driven Breakout

From a technicals standpoint, the stock's price action is highly volatile and event-driven based on the market's perception and pricing of the binary dynamic: transformative potential versus severe dilution. So, the stock may keep consolidating after the sharp pullback caused by the June financing moves. The price trend will remain neutral until a decisive break of either support or resistance. A primary support level exists around the $10.5-$11 zone. This area corresponds to the price of the offering in mid-June and the look-back price for the convertible note. This is suggesting a level where significant buying interest previously emerged. A secondary, more fundamental support level can be considered ~$8.50 that represents the cash price paid for the primary physical asset, the Cedar Port land.

On the upside, a major resistance level is at $14.80, the price of the late-June registered direct offering. At this price, the company itself became a seller by introducing considerable supply into the market. A stable move above this level on high volume, likely triggered by a positive fundamental catalyst like an offtake agreement, can be a strong bullish signal, indicating that shares’ demand has absorbed the prior supply. A secondary resistance stands at the $20 peak reached in mid-June that represents the next major psychological hurdle to overcome.

Final Thoughts: A Speculative Bet With Upside Contingent On Milestone-Driven Execution

To conclude, considering the nature of Houston American Energy's fundamentals, a traditional buy-and-hold strategy would be inappropriate and carries an unacceptably high risk of capital destruction. Here, the company's past performance is irrelevant. Its future depends mostly on the successful execution of a new but unproven business plan. The bullish case offers the potential for high returns, but the bearish case points towards a near-total loss. Therefore, I think the relevant investment method here is a milestone-driven and phased-in approach. This strategy can allow investors to manage risk by putting capital in tranches contingent upon the company achieving specific milestones.

I recommend that investors consider an initial, small speculative position now that the AGIG acquisition is complete and the strategic direction is set. However, any further level of capital allocation should be strictly withheld until the company demonstrates tangible progress with plant construction. My roadmap for a phased investment is detailed in the graphic below.

I believe this method is disciplined and prevents investors from taking actions solely based on the narrative. It instead requires proof of execution before putting in more money in HUSA stock. My investment stance may shift to a bullish view if the company attains successful commissioning of the plant and first operational results with positive gross margin (relating to "Phase 4 Allocation" in the image above).

data.title: The More It Underperforms, The More I Buy
data.description:
Recently, I have been reading a lot of comments on Seeking Alpha indicating that investors are giving up on several proven dividend growth investments, citing the extended period of poor total return performance. Instead, they are increasingly gravitating towards stocks and ETFs that are aligned with the mega-cap technology stocks that are booming from the ongoing AI revolution. In particular, covered call ETFs that have considerable mega-cap tech stock exposure in their underlying portfolios, like the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and others, have become increasingly popular in recent years because of the strong performance of their underlying holdings, coupled with the attractive monthly distributions that they throw off. All we need to look at is the rapid increase in assets under management for the likes of the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) in recent years to show this to be the case.

After all, the reasoning goes, when you can get a higher yield and a higher total return than with many leading dividend growth stocks and ETFs, why continue to invest in those underperforming stocks and ETFs? Instead, jump ship and invest in new, innovative products like JEPI, JEPQ, and others.

In my opinion, as a long-term-oriented value investor, this is the very mentality that signals to me that there is a significant opportunity for contrarian investors. Even more importantly, when looking under the hood at the fundamentals and valuation relative to what you get in these increasingly out-of-favor dividend growth stocks and funds, now looks like the time to be pouncing and loading up on these investments. As a result, the more they underperform and the longer that period of underperformance stretches on, the more I am inclined to buy them.

With this in mind, in this article, I am going to detail two such blue-chip dividend growth investments to illustrate my point.

Blue Chip Underperforming Dividend Opportunity #1

The first one I am going to talk about is Realty Income (O). It is a leading triple net lease REIT that has a vast portfolio of over 15,000 properties spread across the United States, Europe, and a few other countries. Additionally, it is spread across a vast array of industries and generates the vast majority of its rental income from businesses that are either recession-resistant or e-commerce-resistant. Moreover, it generates a large percentage of its revenue from investment-grade tenants.

When combined with the conservative and long-dated nature of triple-net lease REITs, O has an extremely defensive profile. This is evidenced by the fact that it has delivered very consistent cash flow and dividend per share growth over the roughly three decades that it has been in existence. As such, it is a dividend aristocrat and has a stellar track record of delivering stable returns through all sorts of economic environments while also being a very dependable dividend growth machine.

That being said, in recent years, it has delivered a very lackluster total return performance despite continued growth in its dividend and FFO per share over that period of time.

The reasons are not difficult to discover and are largely twofold. First and foremost, interest rates have risen materially over that period of time.

Given its long-dated, stable, and dependable cash flows, O is viewed by many as a bond proxy. As such, with falling bond prices and rising bond yields, O's stock price declines and its yield rises.

The second point is related in that its growth has begun to stagnate. This is due to several factors. First and foremost is the fact that its cost of capital has soared as its stock price has fallen and interest rates have risen. Additionally, its incredibly large size makes it increasingly challenging for it to achieve needle-moving growth via individual property acquisitions. Instead, it now has to chase larger existing portfolios, including taking publicly traded REITs private, to grow its portfolio. As a result, its growth is increasingly less efficient and therefore less attractive than it used to be once upon a time.

That being said, the intrinsic value per share has continued to compound in recent years. As already mentioned, the cash flow and dividend per share have continued to grow, while the balance sheet has remained in solid shape. As a result, buying shares today, you get a higher dividend yield than you would have for much of the past decade, making it a highly compelling buy.

Additionally, while the Fed seems reluctant to cut interest rates—at least for now—it is widely expected that over the next year interest rates will be meaningfully cut. This should provide a powerful tailwind to the stock price if and when it comes to fruition.

When you combine the attractive near 6% forward dividend yield with analyst expectations for inflation-beating dividend and FFO per share growth for the foreseeable future, as well as the potential for valuation multiple expansion—given that it currently trades at a much lower price-to-NAV ratio than it has historically—and the likelihood that the NAV itself will increase as interest rates fall, O looks like a highly compelling buy right now.

As a result, the longer it continues to be a market laggard, the more attractive it becomes as it continues to steadily compound cash flow and dividends per share.

Blue Chip Underperforming Dividend Opportunity #2

The next battle-tested dividend growth machine that I think is very attractive right now, despite recent underperformance, is the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Yes, over the past few years, it has significantly lagged behind the broader market, but this is also very understandable.

Specifically, it is because it does not hold any of the mega-cap tech stocks that have boomed so strongly due to the AI revolution.

However, just because the stock prices of its underlying holdings have not soared higher does not mean that its underlying value as a dividend payer and dividend grower has declined. In fact, nothing could be further from the truth, as it has continued to grow its dividends at a rapid rate over that period of time, and today yields nearly 4%.

In fact, I expect SCHD to begin playing catch-up on the AI front, as many of its holdings—ranging from the likes of PepsiCo (PEP) to Altria (MO) to Home Depot (HD)—begin benefiting significantly from the AI boom. Whether it be through increased automation and other AI advances that dramatically enhance supply chain, production, marketing, and other operational functions, these companies are well-positioned to capitalize on this trend. This is because my view is that the large, blue-chip, well-established businesses that have the largest scale, financial resources, and—most importantly—proprietary databases are going to be the ones that derive the most outsized benefit from AI advances. They will be able to invest most aggressively in R&D as well as in rolling out automation processes, while also having proprietary databases on which they can train the very best business-specific AI models to give themselves an edge over smaller, less data-rich competitors.

Moreover, SCHD's largest sector exposure is to energy (XLE), with ~20% of its portfolio exposed to this segment. Given the bullish indicators for the sector from surging energy demand from the AI boom as well as recently signed trade deals with Japan, the EU, and South Korea that include pledges for hundreds of billions of dollars of energy imports from the United States over the next few years, energy giants like Chevron (CVX) (SCHD's largest position) stand to benefit.

Therefore, funds like SCHD, which have gotten left behind on the valuation front during the AI boom’s early innings, should play significant catch-up in the coming years—both in terms of actual AI benefits flowing through to their underlying holdings' bottom lines and the likelihood that market sentiment will shift once this trend becomes apparent.

As such, I think that right now is a great time to buy SCHD, and the longer it remains a laggard and its dividend yield rises, the more attractive it becomes.

Investor Takeaway

While recently the AI boom has left behind many dividend growth stocks, it has also created an incredible opportunity to buy some of these leading blue chips at very attractive yields and valuations. Between REITs like O and other blue-chip dividend growth stocks such as those held in SCHD's 100-plus stock portfolio, investors have a plethora of opportunities to build attractive and growing passive income streams while also setting themselves up for material outperformance over the long term once market sentiment shifts and the benefits of AI and lower interest rates begin to flow through to the currently out-of-favor dividend growth names. As a result, we are loading up on these types of stocks and funds in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.
